source-discipline.reason: All four pilot probes performed live in session against real URLs: ncc.abcb.gov.au returning HTTP 200 42KB; dibk.no returning 200 214KB; nfpa.org returning 200 260KB; standards.govt.nz shop URL returning 200 961-byte stub. Token counts and HTTP statuses recorded in changelog and migration file are observed, not pattern-matched. Boverket URL guesses all returned 404 — recorded honestly rather than fabricated as a different status.

best-practice-synthesis-routing.reason: Pilot batch wrote to verification_status axis only; no best_practice_synthesis content authored. Phase B work, not Phase E.

adversarial-research-protocol.reason: Rule #7 governs research-generating gap closures (gaps table writes). This batch wrote to evidence_sources verification_status; no gap rows touched. The pilot itself produces empirical findings (4 portal probes) but these are tooling observations, not synthesis claims.

evidence-verification-gate-for-synthesis.reason: The pilot batch directly advances rule #10's verification_status axis. Eligible pool grew 221 -> 225 (+4). Each row was evaluated against §3.2's four criteria explicitly. The UNVERIFIED-1 admission for REF-00081 (NZ) explicitly preserves rule #10's existence gate while flagging the SPA limitation for downstream Pass 3 awareness.

deviations.reason: REF-00081 NZ marked UNVERIFIED-1 rather than VERIFIED because the catalog portal is SPA-rendered and the cited document's identity could not be confirmed from initial static HTML. UNVERIFIED-1 clears the rule #10 existence gate per DR-2026-05-13 §3 (both VERIFIED and UNVERIFIED-1 admit) but represents weaker existence-verification than a static-portal VERIFIED row. The independent-reviewer counterclaim in the DR §5.1 names this exact trade-off.

bias_direction: Same self-authoring bias as the original DR commit. The pilot was authored against a protocol I designed earlier in this same session. Pilot outcomes are reported as I observed them (3 VERIFIED, 1 UNVERIFIED-1), but the choice of pilot rows favored jurisdictions where the protocol was more likely to succeed (AU and NO are known government-portal-friendly jurisdictions). A pilot that started with CN or BR (where probe-feasibility is much weaker) would have produced a less favorable yield ratio.
independent_reviewer_counterclaim: An outside reviewer would object that pilot batch 1 oversampled the easy cases. AU government code with fully static portal is not representative of the 55-row population, which is dominated by SPA-portal jurisdictions (CN, JP, NL, NZ — together 17 of 55 rows). The pilot's 3-VERIFIED / 1-UNVERIFIED-1 yield ratio (75% strict VERIFIED) is likely an upper bound; population-wide yield could be closer to 25-40% strict VERIFIED with the remainder UNVERIFIED-1, NEEDS-HUMAN, or NO-MATCH. The pilot validates the protocol's state-machine logic but does not validate its yield-at-scale.
